sections.content:
Pythagoras' Theorem

In any right-angled triangle, the square of the longest side (the hypotenuse) equals the sum of the squares of the other two sides.

a² + b² = c²

where c is always the hypotenuse (the side opposite the right angle — always the longest side).

Finding the hypotenuse:
If a = 3, b = 4: c² = 3² + 4² = 9 + 16 = 25 → c = √25 = 5

Finding a shorter side:
If c = 13, b = 5: a² = 13² − 5² = 169 − 25 = 144 → a = √144 = 12

🔑 Common Pythagorean triples to remember:
• 3, 4, 5 (and multiples: 6-8-10, 9-12-15)
• 5, 12, 13
• 8, 15, 17
These are integer solutions to a² + b² = c².

Checking for Right Angles

You can check whether a triangle is right-angled by testing if a² + b² = c². If the equation holds true, it's a right-angled triangle; if not, it isn't.

Example: Is a triangle with sides 7, 24, 25 right-angled?
7² + 24² = 49 + 576 = 625 = 25². Yes! It is right-angled.

Applications

Pythagoras' theorem is used in real-world problems such as:
• Finding the diagonal of a rectangle: d² = l² + w²
• Finding the height of a ladder against a wall
• Finding distances on a coordinate grid: distance = √[(x₂−x₁)² + (y₂−y₁)²]

Example: A ladder 10 m long leans against a wall. Its foot is 6 m from the wall. How high up the wall does it reach?
h² = 10² − 6² = 100 − 36 = 64 → h = 8 m
